one_line_takeaway: Freezing the base LLM and confining unlearning updates to merged LoRA adapters (with layer selection and higher learning rates) produces larger-magnitude weight deltas than full-parameter fine-tuning, measurably reducing -- though not eliminating -- the tendency of 4-bit post-training quantization to revert Llama-2-7B/MUSE unlearned models back toward pre-unlearning memorization and privacy leakage.
key_finding: On Llama-2-7B/MUSE, LoRA-based unlearning is more robust to 4-bit PTQ than Full-FT across the utility-regularized methods. Reported paper numbers: for NPO+GDR on BOOKS, Int4 UtilityPres is 50.17 (Full-FT) vs 58.10 (LoRA) -- a 7.93-point improvement, and LoRA's utility is nearly unchanged from full precision (59.65) to Int4 (58.10) versus Full-FT's larger drop (60.09 to 50.17). For GA+GDR on NEWS, Int4 UtilityPres rises from 40.06 (Full-FT) to 44.82 (LoRA), a 4.76-point gain. For GA+KLR on BOOKS, PrivLeak improves from -25.68 (Full-FT, Int4) to -5.86 (LoRA, Int4), moving closer to the ideal value of 0, while VerMem/KnowMem stay near 0 (0.14 and 0.00 respectively) indicating forgetting is preserved. For NPO+KLR on BOOKS, LoRA metrics are nearly identical between full precision and Int4 (VerMem 16.76 to 17.03, UtilityPres 41.82 to 42.02), described by the authors as the most quantization-stable configuration, though on NEWS LoRA does not consistently beat Full-FT in absolute forgetting/utility for this method. The paper also reproduces, as a baseline replication of prior work, that unregularized GA achieves near-zero VerMem/KnowMem at 4-bit only because it collapses utility to ~0, which the authors characterize as a misleading 'success.'
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution is a method for making LLM machine-unlearning survive post-training quantization (PTQ): instead of full-parameter fine-tuning (Full-FT) for unlearning, freeze the base model weights W0 and restrict the unlearning optimization to trainable low-rank adapters (LoRA, B in R^{d x r}, A in R^{r x k}), then explicitly merge the trained adapters into the base weights before quantization. The paper argues (and empirically shows on Llama-2-7B with the MUSE benchmark) that concentrating the unlearning signal into a low-rank subspace, combined with higher learning rates (~1e-4 vs ~1e-5-1e-7 for Full-FT) and layer-selective adapter placement (chosen via grid search over all-linear, MLP-only, and attention-only modules), produces weight updates large enough in magnitude to cross 4-bit quantization bin boundaries, whereas Full-FT's diffuse, small-magnitude updates get masked by the coarse quantization grid and the model reverts toward pre-unlearning behavior. The paper restricts its LoRA experiments to unlearning objectives paired with explicit utility regularization (GDR or KLR), excluding unregularized GA/NPO because those can achieve 'unlearning' only via wholesale utility collapse.
threat_model: Not an adversarial attacker threat model in the traditional security sense; the concern is a privacy/compliance failure mode: an LLM operator runs an unlearning procedure (e.g., to comply with data-deletion/copyright requests) believing sensitive or copyrighted content (Harry Potter novel text in MUSE-BOOKS, BBC news articles in MUSE-NEWS) has been removed, then quantizes the model to 4-bit for efficient deployment. The quantization step can silently 'undo' the unlearning by rounding the small weight deltas back into their original quantization bins, so the deployed (quantized) model regenerates content and answers to queries that should have been forgotten -- a discrepancy between the tested (full-precision) model and the actually-shipped (quantized) model. The paper measures this via privacy leakage under membership-inference-style Min-K% Prob AUC-ROC (PrivLeak metric) as well as verbatim/knowledge memorization.
limitations: Single base model (Llama-2-7B only) and a single benchmark (MUSE, two domains: BOOKS and NEWS); no evaluation on TOFU or other unlearning benchmarks, nor larger/different model families. Only Round-to-Nearest PTQ is run experimentally; GPTQ/AWQ robustness is asserted by citing prior work rather than tested by the authors themselves. Regularization weights (lambda) were tuned on Full-FT and fixed for LoRA, which the authors frame as a controlled-comparison choice but which also means LoRA-specific regularization tuning was not explored. Results are mixed rather than uniformly positive: LoRA sometimes reduces full-precision utility relative to Full-FT (e.g., GA+GDR BOOKS: 68.74 to 61.90) and does not consistently outperform Full-FT on NEWS for NPO+KLR. The paper explicitly excludes unregularized GA/NPO from the LoRA robustness study, so it does not characterize LoRA's effect on those unstable objectives. No formal theoretical proof is given for the 'crosses the quantization boundary' claim -- the argument in Section III/IV is a qualitative/analytical explanation (adopting the framework of prior work, ref [4]) rather than a derived bound tying learning rate, rank, and quantization step size together quantitatively.

datasets_used: MUSE benchmark - BOOKS corpus (Harry Potter novel text as forget set, Harry Potter FanWiki as retain set)
MUSE benchmark - NEWS corpus (BBC news articles, split into forget/retain/holdout sets)
quantitative_results: NPO+GDR, BOOKS, UtilityPres at Int4: Full-FT 50.17 vs LoRA 58.10 (+7.93 points)
GA+GDR, NEWS, UtilityPres at Int4: Full-FT 40.06 vs LoRA 44.82 (+4.76 points)
GA+KLR, BOOKS, PrivLeak at Int4: Full-FT -25.68 vs LoRA -5.86 (closer to ideal 0)
NPO+KLR, BOOKS, LoRA VerMem: full precision 16.76 vs Int4 17.03 (near-stable); UtilityPres 41.82 vs 42.02
GA+GDR, BOOKS, full-precision UtilityPres: Full-FT 68.74 vs LoRA 61.90 (LoRA reduces full-precision utility here)
GA, Full-FT baseline, Int4: VerMem/KnowMem/UtilityPres all 0.00 on BOOKS, attributed to utility collapse rather than genuine robust unlearning
baselines_compared: GA + Gradient Descent on Retain set (GA+GDR)
Each compared as Full-parameter fine-tuning (Full-FT) vs LoRA-adapter variant, under BF16/Int8/Int4 precision
